judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports correctly and thoroughly identify the same root causes and fixes for all three questions; Report 1 is slightly more precise with file/line citations (e.g. explicitly naming docker-entrypoint-initdb.d), while Report 2 adds a bit more forward-looking remediation detail — overall the two are comparable in substance.

report_1.evidence: Responsible file/config: cedarline/.env (and .env.example) — DATABASE_URL points the app at cedar_owner instead of cedar_app... In PostgreSQL, RLS policies are not enforced for superusers, nor for the table owner (the owner is exempt unless FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is set, which it is not here). cedar_owner is both.

report_2.evidence: cedar_owner is the cluster superuser and the object owner... In Postgres, a superuser always bypasses RLS, and a table owner bypasses RLS unless FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is set. cedar_owner is both. Therefore the tenant_isolation policy is never evaluated for the app's connections.

report_1.evidence: Point the app at the least-privilege role. Change DATABASE_URL in .env/.env.example to postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar... Keep two distinct roles, permanently (matches the schema's intent and the two-role model): cedar_owner used only for running migrations (it owns the tables / DDL); cedar_app used for all application runtime traffic.

report_2.evidence: Change the application's connection role to cedar_app. Set DATABASE_URL in .env/.env.example to postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar so the FastAPI app (db.py) connects as the non-owner, non-superuser role that RLS governs. Keep cedar_owner only for migrations (migrate.sh) and the one-time bootstrap.

report_1.evidence: cedar_app is created only by the one-time bootstrap db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql, which is deliberately not part of automatic init (its own header: "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup"... It is not mounted into the container's docker-entrypoint-initdb.d and is not called by scripts/migrate.sh... scripts/reset-local-db.sh:3 runs docker compose down -v, which removes the cedar_pgdata volume... reset-local-db.sh:6 then runs only scripts/migrate.sh (schema), which never touches create_app_role.sql.

report_2.evidence: The cedar_app role is created only by db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql, which is explicitly a one-time manual step... scripts/reset-local-db.sh runs docker compose down -v, which deletes the cedar_pgdata volume (the entire Postgres cluster, including all roles), then docker compose up -d db (fresh cluster with only cedar_owner, created from POSTGRES_USER) and ./scripts/migrate.sh... The reset script never re-runs create_app_role.sql.
